factors.label: Is minimising operational change risk a priority?
factors.description: VSI preserves the familiar VM operating model — no new orchestration layer, no new failure modes to learn.

factors.label: Is your team sysadmin-oriented rather than Kubernetes-native?
factors.description: VSI management uses traditional VM skills. ROKS requires OpenShift/Kubernetes expertise for day-2 operations.

factors.label: Is your VM estate small enough that fixed cluster overhead is a concern?
factors.description: ROKS requires a minimum 3-node cluster regardless of VM count. VSI costs scale linearly with each VM deployed.

factors.label: Do you have existing Ansible, Terraform, or VM-oriented runbooks you want to reuse?
factors.description: VM-oriented automation works directly with VSI. ROKS VMs require adapting runbooks to Kubernetes APIs and operators.

factors.label: Is this a pure lift-convert-shift with no modernization mandate?
factors.description: If containerization isn't required, VSI avoids the complexity of an OpenShift platform you won't fully utilise.

factors.label: Are you concerned about cluster-level blast radius affecting all VMs simultaneously?
factors.description: A VSI failure is isolated to one VM. An ROKS cluster issue (API server, ODF storage) can affect all VMs at once.

factors.label: Does your compliance framework assume traditional VM isolation boundaries?
factors.description: VSI provides familiar hypervisor-level isolation. ROKS uses namespace and pod security boundaries that audit teams may need to re-evaluate.

factors.label: Does your team lack experience with distributed storage (e.g. Ceph/ODF)?
factors.description: ROKS uses ODF (Ceph-based) for VM storage, requiring specialist knowledge for capacity planning and incident response.

factors.label: Are 3rd party tools and scripting acceptable for migration?
factors.description: VSI migration uses established tools (Wanclouds VPC+, Rackware RMM, custom scripts). ROKS provides integrated migration via MTV/Forklift but requires cluster readiness first.

factors.label: Plan to containerize workloads during or after migration
factors.description: ROKS/OpenShift Virtualization can run VMs alongside containers, enabling gradual modernization.

factors.label: Kubernetes/OpenShift skills available in the team
factors.description: Teams with Kubernetes experience can leverage OpenShift Virtualization immediately.

factors.label: Desire for a unified platform for VMs and containers
factors.description: ROKS runs both VMs and containers on a single control plane, simplifying operations.

factors.label: CI/CD pipelines and GitOps workflows in place or planned
factors.description: OpenShift integrates natively with GitOps, Tekton, and ArgoCD for automated deployments.

factors.label: Need for service mesh, observability, or advanced networking
factors.description: OpenShift provides built-in service mesh, monitoring, and software-defined networking.

factors.label: Auto-scaling or dynamic workload placement requirements
factors.description: Kubernetes-native auto-scaling and scheduling optimize resource utilization automatically.

factors.label: Multi-cloud or hybrid cloud strategy with consistent tooling
factors.description: OpenShift provides a consistent platform across on-prem, IBM Cloud, and other clouds.

factors.label: Is cost an important factor in your platform decision?
factors.description: When answered Yes, this factor favours whichever platform has lower estimated monthly cost. Configure costs on the ROKS and VSI Migration pages.

notes: For workloads requiring IBM Power architecture or very high single-VM memory (>512 GiB), consider PowerVS connected via Transit Gateway.
